---
title: "Solutions â€” Exercise Sheet 9: Optical Transitions and Lasers"
subtitle: "EP4: Experimental Physics 4 â€” Atomic and Molecular Physics"
---

## Solution 1: Einstein Coefficients

**(a)** In thermal equilibrium with radiation density $\rho(\nu)$, the rate equations give:

$N_1 B_{12}\rho = N_2(A_{21} + B_{21}\rho)$

Using $N_2/N_1 = (g_2/g_1)e^{-h\nu/(k_BT)}$ and requiring $\rho(\nu)$ to equal the Planck distribution:

$$
\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21}/B_{21}}{(g_1 B_{12})/(g_2 B_{21})e^{h\nu/(k_BT)} - 1}
$$

Comparing with $\rho(\nu) = \frac{8\pi h\nu^3/c^3}{e^{h\nu/(k_BT)} - 1}$ gives:

$B_{12}/B_{21} = g_2/g_1$ and $A_{21}/B_{21} = 8\pi h\nu^3/c^3$. âś“

**(b)** $A_{21}/B_{21} = 8\pi h\nu^3/c^3 = 8\pi \times 6.626\times10^{-34}\times(2.47\times10^{15})^3/(3\times10^8)^3$

$= 8\pi \times 6.626\times10^{-34}\times 1.506\times10^{46}/2.7\times10^{25} = 7.3\times10^{-13}\;\text{JÂ·s/m}^3$

The large $\nu^3$ dependence means that at optical frequencies, spontaneous emission dominates over stimulated emission unless the radiation field is very intense.

**(c)** Lifetime: $\tau = 1/A_{21} = 1/(6.27\times10^8) = 1.60\;\text{ns}$

Natural linewidth: $\Delta\nu_{\text{nat}} = A_{21}/(2\pi) = 99.8\;\text{MHz} \approx 100\;\text{MHz}$

**(d)** $\tau_{2s} = 1/8.2 = 0.122\;\text{s}$. This is $\sim 10^8$ times longer than the $2p$ lifetime. The $2s$ state is "metastable" because it cannot decay by the dominant (E1) channel and lives extremely long compared to other excited states.

## Solution 2: Selection Rules

**(a)**
- $3d \to 2p$: $\Delta\ell = -1$ â†’ **allowed** âś“
- $3d \to 2s$: $\Delta\ell = -2$ â†’ **forbidden** âś—
- $3s \to 2s$: $\Delta\ell = 0$ â†’ **forbidden** âś—
- $4f \to 3d$: $\Delta\ell = -1$ â†’ **allowed** âś“
- $4f \to 2p$: $\Delta\ell = -2$ â†’ **forbidden** âś—

**(b)** The $^3P_1 \to\, ^1S_0$ transition requires $\Delta S = -1$ (triplet to singlet), which violates the $\Delta S = 0$ selection rule. It is only weakly allowed due to spinâ€“orbit coupling mixing singlet and triplet states. In light atoms like helium where spinâ€“orbit coupling is weak, the line is extremely faint.

**(c)** The M1 rate for $2s \to 1s$ is essentially zero for hydrogen (it is strictly forbidden by $\Delta\ell = 0$). The two-photon E1 rate is $\sim 8.2\;\text{s}^{-1}$, so the two-photon process completely dominates.

## Solution 3: Laser Threshold and Mode Structure

**(a)** $\Delta\nu_{\text{FSR}} = c/(2L) = 3\times10^8/(2\times0.3) = 500\;\text{MHz}$

**(b)** Number of modes: $N = \Delta\nu_g/\Delta\nu_{\text{FSR}} = 120\times10^9/500\times10^6 = 240$ modes.

**(c)** $R_1 R_2\,e^{2gL} = 1$, so $2gL = -\ln(R_1 R_2) = -\ln(0.999\times0.95) = -\ln(0.94905) = 0.05232$

$g = 0.05232/(2\times0.3) = 0.0872\;\text{m}^{-1} = 0.087\;\text{m}^{-1}$

## Solution 4: Three-Level vs. Four-Level Laser

**(a)** In a three-level system, the total number of atoms is $N = N_1 + N_2$ (level 3 depopulates instantly). For gain, we need $N_2 > N_1$, i.e., $N_2 > N/2$. More than half of all atoms must be in the upper laser level. This requires high pump power.

**(b)** In a four-level system, the lower laser level (level 2) is rapidly emptied to level 1 by non-radiative decay. Thus $N_2 \approx 0$, and any small population in level 3 gives inversion ($N_3 > N_2 \approx 0$). The threshold pump power is dramatically reduced.

**(c)** At threshold, $N_2 = N/2 = 5\times10^{19}$. The pump must compensate spontaneous emission:

$P_{\text{pump}} = N_2 \cdot h\nu \cdot A_{21} = 5\times10^{19}\times6.626\times10^{-34}\times4.32\times10^{14}\times300$

$= 5\times10^{19}\times2.863\times10^{-19}\times 300 = 4.3\;\text{kW}$

This is a substantial power, explaining why three-level lasers (like ruby) require powerful flashlamp pumping.
